rows.title: Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act 1977: Federal Framework for Review of Commonwealth Administrative Decisions
rows.summary:
The Administrative Decisions (Judicial Review) Act 1977 (Cth) (the ADJR Act) is a Commonwealth statute of Australia that establishes a statutory framework for judicial review of decisions made under Commonwealth law by federal officers and authorities. The Act is administered at the federal level and applies throughout Australia.

Enacted by the Australian Parliament and receiving Royal Assent on 16 June 1977, the ADJR Act came into operation on 1 October 1980. The Act created a simplified, codified pathway for persons aggrieved by administrative decisions to seek review in the Federal Court of Australia, and subsequently also in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia.

The Act provides, under section 5, that a person who is aggrieved by a decision to which the Act applies may apply to the Federal Court for an order of review on one or more of the grounds set out in that section. Those grounds include that the decision-maker acted without jurisdiction, failed to observe procedures required by law, denied natural justice, made an error of law, or that the decision was induced or affected by fraud. Section 6 extends equivalent grounds of review to conduct engaged in for the purpose of making a decision, and section 7 addresses failures to make a decision within a required time. Under section 13, an applicant is entitled to obtain a written statement of reasons for a decision to which the Act applies, a provision that has been utilised extensively in Commonwealth administrative law practice.

Schedule 1 to the Act lists classes of decisions that are excluded from the Act's operation, including decisions made under the Criminal Code and certain decisions under the Migration Act 1958 (Cth), reflecting the legislature's determination to carve out specific categories from the statutory review regime. The Act operates alongside, and does not exclude, the constitutional jurisdiction of the High Court of Australia under section 75(v) of the Constitution to grant writs of mandamus, prohibition, and certiorari against officers of the Commonwealth.

The ADJR Act has been amended on numerous occasions since its commencement, with amendments addressing changes to the federal court structure, the scope of reviewable decisions, and alignment with other Commonwealth administrative law instruments including the Administrative Review Tribunal Act 2024 (Cth), which replaced the Administrative Appeals Tribunal.

rows.title: OAIC and eSafety Commissioner Formalise Cooperation on Privacy and Online Safety via MOU
rows.summary:
The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner and the eSafety Commissioner have entered into a Memorandum of Understanding to coordinate regulatory responses where privacy and online safety obligations intersect, with particular focus on age assurance requirements and social media minimum age compliance.

On 23 April 2026, the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) and the eSafety Commissioner announced the execution of a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) formalising cooperation and information sharing between the two regulators. The OAIC indicates that the agreement builds on existing collaboration between the agencies and reflects the convergence of regulatory responsibilities under applicable Australian law, including obligations set out in the Online Safety Act.

The OAIC notes that the MOU establishes formal communication pathways between the two regulators on matters where privacy rights and online safety requirements overlap. The agreement covers age assurance requirements now mandatory under Australia's online industry codes and standards, which the OAIC indicates are designed to protect children from abuse and age-inappropriate material, as well as compliance by age-restricted platforms with Social Media Minimum Age obligations. Australian Information Commissioner Elizabeth Tydd states that the MOU will advance work led by Privacy Commissioner Carly Kind and assist the OAIC in monitoring and responding to emerging online privacy risks, and in delivering on both agencies' statutory functions as set out in the Online Safety Act.

eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant states that the implementation of the Social Media Minimum Age regime required recognition of privacy rights from the outset, and that the MOU gives formal recognition to that principle. The eSafety Commissioner further states that the proliferation of technologies such as artificial intelligence is amplifying online risks, and that industry is increasingly required to deploy age-assurance technologies that meet regulatory obligations while respecting privacy in the Australian context. The OAIC indicates that through information and expertise sharing under the MOU, both agencies intend to address privacy and safety challenges in the digital environment through coordinated regulatory action.
rows.body_name: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner

rows.title: Australian Privacy Commissioner Finds InspectRealEstate's 2Apply Platform Breached Privacy Principles Over Excessive and Unfair Personal Information Collection
rows.summary:
The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner has determined that IRE Pty Ltd, trading as InspectRealEstate, interfered with the privacy of individuals through its 2Apply rental application platform, in breach of Australian Privacy Principles 3.2 and 3.5 under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), with no monetary penalty imposed but remedial declarations issued.

On 1 April 2026, Privacy Commissioner Carly Kind issued determination [2026] AICmr 24 (case reference CII25/00001), concluding a year-long commissioner-initiated investigation into IRE Pty Ltd. The determination finds that IRE's 2Apply platform interfered with the privacy of individuals whose personal information was collected between March 2020 and 18 March 2025, within the meaning of s 13(1) of the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth). No financial penalty is recorded in the determination; IRE agreed to adapt its practices on a without-admissions basis.

The Privacy Commissioner finds that 2Apply collected personal information that is not reasonably necessary for IRE's functions or activities, in breach of APP 3.2, including data on prospective renters' gender, student status, citizenship status, visa expiry date, and previous living history. The Commissioner further determines that IRE collected personal information by unfair means in breach of APP 3.5, citing the material power imbalance between renters and real estate agents, property managers, and landlords, exacerbated by the prevailing rental crisis. Under s 52(1A)(a) and s 52(1A)(b) of the Privacy Act, IRE is required to cease collecting the specified categories of personal information within 60 days of the determination date, and is required to engage an independent privacy reviewer, at its own expense, to assess its collection practices, online choice architecture, and data retention policies (APP 11.2).

In a first for the OAIC, the determination applies the concept of online choice architecture to evaluate the design and structure of the 2Apply form. The Commissioner finds that the form deployed confirmshaming, biased framing, and bundled consent, each of which the determination concludes unfairly pressured individuals into disclosing personal information inconsistent with their preferences. The Commissioner's determination emphasises that other RentTech providers are expected to align their practices with the findings, and the OAIC has provided the decision to real estate peak bodies to assist agents, property managers, and landlords in considering its implications for their own information collection practices.
rows.body_name: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner

rows.title: ASIC Sets Out Enforcement and Surveillance Framework for Illegal Phoenix Activity
rows.summary:
The Australian Securities and Investments Commission has published details of its regulatory framework targeting illegal phoenix activity, encompassing engagement, surveillance and enforcement tools directed at company directors, service providers and registered liquidators under Australian corporations law.

On 7 January 2026, the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) released a detailed statement of its current approach to identifying, disrupting and taking action against illegal phoenix activity. No fine or penalty was announced in connection with this publication. ASIC states that its regulatory focus is directed at director, service provider and registered liquidator misconduct that harms creditors and consumers and reduces business confidence.

ASIC describes illegal phoenix activity as occurring when company directors abandon a company or transfer its business to a new entity without paying true or market value, leaving debts with the old company, which is then placed into liquidation or abandoned. ASIC finds that where a liquidator is appointed in such circumstances, the absence of recoverable assets means creditors cannot be paid. ASIC states it uses a combination of engagement, surveillance and enforcement tools to address this conduct.

On engagement, ASIC states it shares and refers information to other government agencies, including the Serious Financial Crime Taskforce and the Phoenix Taskforce, and exchanges intelligence with the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) as part of the Data Fusion Project to detect illegal phoenix activity. ASIC also states it supports and implements legislative tools introduced to combat this activity and contributes to law reform and policy.

On surveillance, ASIC states it operates a Phoenix Surveillance Campaign, working with the ATO to identify high-risk directors and jointly conducting meetings with directors considered at risk of engaging in illegal phoenix activity. ASIC also states it conducts registered liquidator surveillance, including consideration of whether to appoint a reviewing liquidator funded from the Assetless Administration Fund. On enforcement, ASIC states it takes civil and criminal action, administrative action including director disqualification, and pursues prosecution of directors through the Assistance for External Administration programme, alongside grant funding to liquidators from the Assetless Administration Fund and administrative action against registered liquidators.
rows.body_name: Australian Securities and Investments Commission Press Releases
